content:
11 min read
Just now

--

From “it works on my machine” to a container anyone in the world can run in one command
Press enter or click to view image in full size

From “it works on my machine” to a container anyone in the world can run in one command
Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a very specific kind of pain every developer eventually feels.
You spend weeks building a program. Code is clean. The accuracy is respectable. You push it to GitHub, send the link to a teammate, and say the fatal words: “just run it.”
Twenty minutes later, they message you: ModuleNotFoundError. Then: "Wait, which Python version?" Then: "scikit-learn is throwing a pickle version warning." Then they give up, and you end up screen-sharing for an hour.
Your model didn’t fail. Your environment failed to travel.
That’s the whole problem Docker solves, and after spending an evening with this crash course, I finally understand why every job description asks for it. Here’s everything, in the order it actually clicked for me.

1. So what actually is Docker?
The one-line version: Docker packages your application together with everything it needs to run — code, runtime, libraries, system dependencies, config — into a single portable unit called a container.
The mental model that helped me most is shipping containers. Before standardized shipping containers, moving goods meant every port, truck, and ship handled cargo differently. Then someone said: put everything in an identical steel box. Suddenly, the box’s contents stopped mattering — cranes, ships, and trucks only had to understand the box.
Docker did that for software. It doesn’t care whether your box contains a Flask app, a Postgres database, or an XGBoost model. It just moves the box, and the box behaves identically everywhere.
The tagline that stuck with me: Docker doesn’t fix “it works on my machine.” It makes “my machine” something you can ship.

2. Why do developers specifically need this
Web devs need Docker. Software engineers really need Docker. A few reasons that came up:
The dependency graph is brutal. A typical project isn’t just Python or any language. It’s a specific Python version, a specific NumPy pinned against a specific scikit-learn, maybe CUDA drivers, maybe a system-level library like libgomp that a wheel silently depends on. Any one mismatch and your .pkl file refuses to load.
Models are version-sensitive in a sneaky way. Pickle a model with scikit-learn 1.3 and unpickle it with 1.5, and you may get a warning today and a wrong prediction tomorrow. Freezing the environment isn’t a nice-to-have — it’s correctness.
Dev and prod are different planets. You train on macOS. Production is Ubuntu on some cloud VM. Without containers, that gap is where deployments go to die.
Teams need reproducibility. “Works for me” is not a scientific result. If a reviewer can’t reproduce your training run, your numbers are folklore.
Isolation. Project A needs TensorFlow 2.10. Project B needs 2.16. On one machine, that’s a fight. In containers, they never even meet.
The old answer to all this was virtual machines — but a VM ships an entire guest operating system with it. That’s gigabytes of overhead and a slow boot for what is basically “please run my Flask app.” Containers share the host’s kernel and only package what’s above it. Same isolation benefit, a fraction of the weight, and they start in seconds.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

3. The five words you need to know
This is the part where Docker stops feeling mysterious. There are really only five moving pieces, and they relate to each other in a straight line.
Docker Engine
The thing that actually does the work. It has three parts:

• Docker CLI — what you type. docker run, docker build, etc.

• REST API — how the CLI talks to the daemon.

• Docker Daemon (dockerd) — the background process that builds images, runs containers, and manages everything.
You type into the CLI, it sends a request to the daemon, and the daemon does the real work. That’s it. The CLI is a remote control, not the TV.
Docker Image
A read-only blueprint. It’s your app plus its entire environment, frozen. Think of it as a class in OOP — a definition, not a running thing.
Images are built in layers, and this is the detail worth caring about. Each instruction in your build creates a layer, and layers are cached and shared. If ten of your projects use python:3.11-slim, that base layer is stored once. Change one line of your app code and only the layers after that line get rebuilt. This is why a well-ordered Dockerfile rebuilds in 5 seconds and a badly ordered one takes 5 minutes.
Dockerfile
The recipe that produces an image. A plain text file, usually a dozen lines, that says: start from this base, install these packages, copy this code, run this command.
Docker Container
A running instance of an image. If the image is the class, the container is the object. You can spin up ten containers from one image and they won’t know about each other.
Containers are ephemeral by design. Kill one, start it again, and anything you wrote inside is gone unless you deliberately mounted a volume. This feels scary for about a day and then feels like a feature.
Registry
Where images live so other people (and other machines) can get them. Docker Hub is the default public one — it’s basically GitHub for images. There are private registries too (AWS ECR, GCR, Azure ACR) for when your model shouldn’t be public.

4. The lifecycle, end to end
Here’s the loop everything else fits into:
Write a recipe. Bake it into a blueprint. Run the blueprint. Publish the blueprint. Someone across the world pulls it and gets byte-identical behavior.
Containers themselves have their own small lifecycle: created → running → paused → stopped → deleted. Worth knowing that docker stop and docker rm are different things — stopping keeps the container around (and its filesystem), removing deletes it for good.

5. Where this actually gets used
Not theory — these are the real reasons Docker shows up in job posts:

• Model serving. Wrap the model in a Flask/FastAPI app, containerize it, ship it. The container is your deployment artifact.

• Reproducible research. Publish the image alongside the paper. Reviewers run one command instead of fighting with your requirements file.

• CI/CD. Your tests run in the same container that production runs. No environment drift.

• Microservices. Preprocessing service, model service, and API gateway as three containers, each scaling independently.

• Cloud portability. Every major cloud runs containers. Build once, deploy to AWS, GCP, Azure, or a Raspberry Pi.

• Onboarding. New teammate’s day-one setup goes from a two-page README to docker compose up.

6. Setting it up
Install Docker Desktop from docker.com — it exists for Windows, macOS, and Linux and bundles the engine, CLI, and a GUI.
Windows users: this is the one platform with a real prerequisite. Docker needs WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux). Enable virtualization in your BIOS, enable WSL from Windows Features, install the WSL 2 kernel update, then install Docker Desktop. If you skip this and hit a cryptic error, that’s why.
Mac users on Apple Silicon: grab the Apple Silicon build, not Intel.
Verifying it works
docker --version
docker info
docker run hello-worldThat last one is the real test. It pulls a tiny image from Docker Hub and runs it. If you see the “Hello from Docker!” message, congratulations — you just completed the entire pull → run pipeline without realizing it.
A few commands you’ll use constantly:
docker ps # running containers
docker ps -a # all containers, including stopped
docker images # images on your machine
docker rm <container> # delete a container
docker rmi <image> # delete an image
docker logs <container> # see what your app printed
docker exec -it <container> bash # shell into a running containerThat docker exec -it ... bash one is the debugging lifeline. When your container "runs but doesn't work," go inside and look around.

7. Running someone else’s project
Before building your own image, it’s worth pulling one that already exists — it makes the whole thing concrete.
docker pull <username>/<image-name>
docker run -p 5000:5000 <username>/<image-name>The flag that confused me for a full ten minutes: -p 5000:5000.
Containers are isolated. That includes their network. Your Flask app is listening on port 5000 inside the container, but that port is invisible from your browser until you explicitly map it. The syntax is -p HOST:CONTAINER. So -p 8080:5000 means "when I hit localhost:8080, forward it to port 5000 inside the container."
If your app starts fine but localhost:5000 shows nothing, it's almost always one of two things: you forgot -p, or your Flask app is bound to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0. More on that below, because it bit me.

8. Dockerizing a Flask ML app
This is the main event. The course uses a laptop price predictor — a regression model wrapped in a Flask app (repo here) —, but the pattern is identical for any model.
The project structure:
laptop-price-predictor/
├── app.py
├── model.pkl
├── requirements.txt
└── DockerfileThe Dockerfile
# 1. Start from a slim Python base
FROM python:3.11-slim# 2. Set the working directory inside the container
WORKDIR /app# 3. Copy requirements FIRST (this ordering matters — see below)
COPY requirements.txt .# 4. Install dependencies
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt# 5. Now copy the rest of the app
COPY . .# 6. Document which port the app uses
EXPOSE 5000# 7. The command that runs when the container starts
CMD ["python", "app.py"]Line by line, because every one of these earns its place:
FROM python:3.11-slim — Every image starts from a base. slim is the sweet spot: it's a few hundred MB instead of ~1GB for the full image, but unlike alpine it won't make you fight to compile NumPy. For ML work, start with slim.
WORKDIR /app — creates and moves into /app inside the container. Every subsequent command runs from here. Without it, you're littering files into /.
COPY requirements.txt . before COPY . . — this is the layer caching trick, and it's the single highest-leverage line in the file. Docker caches each layer. If you copy your whole project first and then install dependencies, every single code change invalidates the cache and reinstalls pandas from scratch. By copying only requirements.txt first, the expensive pip install layer stays cached until your dependencies actually change. Rebuild time drops from minutes to seconds.
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir — --no-cache-dir tells pip not to keep its download cache inside the image. That cache is useless in a container and can add hundreds of MB.
EXPOSE 5000 — mostly documentation. It does not publish the port; you still need -p at runtime. It's a note to whoever reads your Dockerfile.
CMD ["python", "app.py"] — the default command. Use the JSON array form (exec form), not CMD python app.py. The shell form wraps your process in /bin/sh, which swallows signals — meaning docker stop won't shut your app down gracefully.
The one-line change in app.py that everyone misses
if __name__ == "__main__":
app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000)host="0.0.0.0" is mandatory. Flask's default is 127.0.0.1, which inside a container means "only listen to traffic from inside this container." Your port mapping will be perfect, and you'll still get connection refused. Bind to 0.0.0.0 so the container accepts traffic from outside.
Don’t skip the .dockerignore
__pycache__/
*.pyc
venv/
.git/
.ipynb_checkpoints/
data/raw/
notebooks/
.envSame idea as .gitignore. Without it, COPY . . cheerfully copies your 2GB raw dataset, your virtualenv, and your entire git history into the image. Your 400MB image becomes 4GB and you wonder why the push takes forever.
Build and run
# Build (the . at the end is the build context — don't forget it)
docker build -t laptop-price-predictor .# Run
docker run -p 5000:5000 laptop-price-predictorThe -t flag tags
